_authoring_notes: v3 first-pass rubric (no prior v2 on main). Authored via the scaleout author↔Gemini-critic loop under the strict-provenance bar: every scored goal must trace to a verbatim or paraphrased user message (instruction.md, user_simulation_prompt.md, or oracle_intents.json). Oracle-specific identifiers the user never voiced are stripped unless the user pinned them verbatim. Weights reflect user emphasis, not test_manifest.yaml F2P gate weights.

Gemini critic final: verdict=REVISE score=0.875 round=3.
strength: Excellent handling of the user's mid-session redirect, correctly adjusting the evaluation to not require exhaustive coverage across the codebase.
strength: Strong weight justification that correctly prioritizes the user's primary behavioral ask over the F2P manifest's heavy weighting of the specific location.
strength: Comprehensive anti-cheating clauses in every goal that specifically target hardcoded strings and discarded results.

residual risk: The rubric strictly requires a 'warning-level' log, which would wrongly reject a valid error-level log despite the user explicitly stating 'at least, log a warning message', and it uses the oracle-specific name 'opencode' which the user never named verbatim.

_task_quality_flags.internet_with_live_origin: task.toml sets allow_internet=true. goal_0 anti-gaming guard names git-ops exfiltration as a zeroing trigger where applicable.

completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine implementation that adds real warning-logging behavior to the session-data error paths, not a gaming artifact. It must FAIL if the diff is empty / a no-op, if it only adds a captured-tree banner (e.g. a diff whose sole content is a '=== repo/ ===' style file-tree dump), if it performs git-ops exfiltration instead of editing code (e.g. `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, restoring files to recover the upstream fix), if the only changes are comments or whitespace, if logging statements are commented out or otherwise dead/unreachable, if a logging call is added but its result is discarded so nothing is ever emitted, or if the change exists only in a temporary/scratch/unmerged location rather than the application's source files. A real fix introduces an actual log emission call on the error branch that executes at runtime.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Calibrated against an empty/golden-only evidence tier where the cheapest fake is a zero-edit or captured-tree diff. Ensures the judged behavior is a live log emission, not a stub or exfiltration of the upstream commit.

completeness_goals.goal: When the code reads session data and an error occurs while decoding/unmarshaling it (rather than silently swallowing the error and continuing), a warning-level (or higher-severity, e.g. error-level — the user said 'at least' a warning) diagnostic is logged so the failure is observable. The log must actually be emitted on the error path at runtime; a parsed-but-unused error value, a TODO, or a commented-out log does not satisfy this. The exact logger function, package, or message wording is unconstrained — any equivalent warning-or-error-level emission is acceptable. Exhaustive coverage of all such locations across the codebase is not required; demonstrating this logging behavior at one or more valid session-data unmarshal error sites (such as the session-read location the user pointed to in the Turn 1 redirect) is sufficient to fully satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 verbatim: 'if we catch any error unmarshaling session data we should, at least, log a warning message'. This is the central ask — surface previously-swallowed unmarshal errors as warnings.

completeness_goals.goal: The emitted log carries enough detail to diagnose the failure — at minimum it includes the underlying error itself AND the offending content (the data that failed to decode) or an equivalent contextual identifier of what was being read. A bare log message with no error or content detail (e.g. just 'parse failed' with no fields) does not satisfy this. Hardcoded error strings or dummy/placeholder content values logged instead of the actual runtime error and data variables do not satisfy this. The field names, structured-vs-formatted style, and ordering are unconstrained.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 verbatim qualifier: 'log a warning message WITH THE CONTENT'. The user explicitly required the content (and implicitly the error) to be in the message, not just a generic warning.

completeness_goals.goal: The warning logging is applied to the session-read error handler the user pointed to in the Turn 1 redirect — i.e. the agent-session read path where extracting the list of modified files from a decoded session can fail non-fatally and was previously swallowed silently. After the fix, that specific failure branch logs a warning-or-error diagnostic instead of silently discarding the failure. Adding the log only to unrelated locations while leaving this one silent does not satisfy this goal; a stub, hardcoded/placeholder log, or commented-out code here does not satisfy it either.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1 redirect: 'add warning logging there' — the user explicitly converged the agent onto the opencode session-read error site (the non-fatal modified-files extraction failure) per user_simulation_prompt.md Turn 2, making this the single location the session ultimately required.

decomposition_notes: Three substantive user asks across turns. Turn 0 carries two distinct requirements that the user stated in one sentence: (a) log a warning when unmarshaling session data fails [goal_1, the broadest and most-weighted ask], and (b) include 'the content' in that warning [goal_2, an explicit qualifier the user spelled out]. Turn 1 ('add warning logging there') is a redirect that converged the agent onto one specific location — the opencode session-read / modified-files-extraction error handler [goal_3]; per the user-sim, the session ultimately committed with only that location changed, so it is weighted as important but below the general behavioral ask. Turn 2 ('commit it') is a process directive with no behavioral payload and is intentionally NOT a goal. Weights rank by user-importance, not by the test_manifest F2P weights: the general 'warn on unmarshal error' behavior (goal_1, 0.45) outranks the content-detail qualifier (goal_2, 0.30), which outranks the specific opencode site (goal_3, 0.25) — even though the manifest weights opencode-specific gates most heavily, the user's primary directive was the general logging behavior, and the opencode focus only emerged as a mid-session redirect. Build/compilation success is treated as an implicit well-formedness property of a genuine edit (covered by goal_0's 'executes at runtime' clause) rather than a standalone process goal. No oracle-specific identifiers (function/file/variable names) appear in goal text; behaviors are described by what the workspace must do. Weighted goals sum to 1.00.
